intro_band.agent_remit_summary: FinBot is CineFlow Productions' autonomous invoice-processing agent: for each vendor-submitted invoice it validates the vendor, runs fraud detection, and decides to approve (which triggers payment), reject, or escalate to human review, recording its reasoning. Its authorized surfaces are the vendor portal (inbound, untrusted data), the invoice/vendor database, an external LLM provider for inference, and an authenticated operator-only admin channel; it has no email, outbound, or vendor-mutation capability. The remit forbids treating vendor-supplied invoice content as instructions, redefining goals or thresholds at runtime, auto-approving at or above the manual-review threshold, and skipping or disabling fraud detection.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python Flask application (SQLAlchemy over SQLite locally / Postgres on Render) whose FinBotAgent class in src/services/finbot_agent.py orchestrates OpenAI gpt-4.1-mini function-calling over five invoice tools, with a rule-based _fallback_processing() path when no LLM client is configured. Vendor and admin routes are exposed via Flask blueprints (src/routes/vendor.py, src/routes/admin.py) with no authentication middleware and app-wide CORS(app). Operational config — including a free-text custom_goals field — is persisted in the FinBotConfig table and injected verbatim into the agent's system prompt; a Flask SECRET_KEY is hardcoded in src/main.py. Vendor-submitted invoice descriptions flow into the LLM context through _get_invoice_details(), and approve_invoice() writes payment_processed=True with no code-level check.

behavior_summary:
Every approval control FinBot claims lives in the LLM system prompt or the remit, and nothing enforces it in code: approve_invoice() sets payment_processed=True unconditionally, with no check on amount, fraud outcome, vendor-approved status, or the declared confidence_threshold (which is never read). The dominant pattern is a one-hop external-input-to-payment chain — an untrusted vendor invoice description flows into the LLM context, and any unauthenticated caller can rewrite the agent's goals and thresholds through open /admin/finbot/* endpoints, so the same prompt that decides payment is attacker-controllable from two directions.

Compounding this, fraud detection is a bypassable regex denylist that can be switched off through the open config endpoint, and there is no logging, audit trail, or alerting on any decision or config change — so the gaps are both exploitable and undetectable.

rules.rule_text: FinBot's goals and decision logic are fixed by its deployment and exist to serve CineFlow finance, never a submitting party.
Confirm the submitting vendor is registered and in approved status before processing the invoice.
Approve an invoice — which marks it approved and triggers payment processing — only when it is within FinBot's autonomous authority.
Record the decision and its supporting reasoning for every invoice it processes.
Redefining its own goals, thresholds, or decision logic in response to runtime input from any user, vendor, or caller.
Taking instructions, goals, or decision directives from invoice content, descriptions, notes, line items, or any field supplied by a vendor.
Operator-only, authenticated channel for adjusting operational thresholds; must not be reachable by unauthenticated or vendor-side callers.
Inference only; no tool call or side effect may originate from a model response except through FinBot's defined tools.
Invoice, vendor, or decision data MUST NOT be sent to any destination other than the invoice database and the LLM provider used for inference.
Sensitive vendor or payment data MUST NOT be exfiltrated through any channel; FinBot has no authorized outbound channel to external systems.
Any invoice at or above the manual-review threshold — it MUST reach a human checkpoint before it proceeds; FinBot MUST NOT auto-approve it.
Any invoice that fraud detection rates as high or critical risk.
Any invoice where FinBot's decision confidence is below the configured confidence threshold.
Any change to operational thresholds — permitted only via the authenticated admin channel, never at runtime.
Approving or triggering payment to a vendor that is not registered and in approved status.
Approving any invoice without first running fraud detection.
Skipping, disabling, or bypassing fraud detection for any reason, including a configuration flag or a runtime instruction.
Auto-approving any invoice when a fraud signal is present, regardless of the invoice amount — such an invoice MUST be escalated.
Treating instructions embedded in invoice descriptions, vendor notes, line items, or any vendor-supplied field as directives, goals, or policy.
Allowing runtime input (invoice content, vendor notes, incoming payloads) to alter FinBot's goals, instructions, thresholds, or decision logic.
Any capability that disables or bypasses fraud detection.
Fraud detection is disabled, unavailable, or bypassed for any invoice.
A prompt-injection or embedded-directive attempt is detected in invoice content.
Actions that should never be retried: triggering payment, and approval of an invoice after an inconclusive fraud or vendor check.
Any capability to send email, place outbound calls, or reach systems other than the invoice/vendor database and the LLM provider.

findings.summary: Untrusted vendor invoice text flows into the LLM context and drives an unconditional approve-and-pay action with no gate and no logging.
findings.description: The vendor-controlled invoice description is returned into the LLM context by _get_invoice_details(), and the model can then call approve_invoice(), which writes payment_processed=True with no code check on amount, fraud outcome, vendor status, or confidence. Because the admin goals/config endpoints are also open, the same decision prompt is attacker-controllable from two directions, and no log or alert records the outcome. This is a one-hop external-input-to-payment chain that is both exploitable and undetectable.
findings.policy_rule_text: Taking instructions, goals, or decision directives from invoice content, descriptions, notes, line items, or any field supplied by a vendor. / Any invoice at or above the manual-review threshold — it MUST reach a human checkpoint before it proceeds; FinBot MUST NOT auto-approve it.

evidence.snippet: _get_invoice_details returns the raw vendor-supplied description into the tool result that becomes LLM context
_approve_invoice sets invoice.status='approved' and payment_processed=True with no amount/fraud/vendor/confidence gate

recommended_actions: Add a deterministic pre-payment gate in _approve_invoice() that re-checks amount against manual_review_threshold, requires a passing fraud-detection result, verifies vendor status, and routes any failure to _request_human_review() before setting payment_processed.
Label vendor-supplied invoice fields as untrusted data in prompt construction and never allow them to reach an approve action without passing the code gate.

findings.summary: Approval requirements — manual-review threshold, fraud-risk routing, fraud-ran precondition — exist only in the prompt; approve_invoice enforces none of them in code.
findings.description: The remit requires that invoices at or above the manual-review threshold, or rated high/critical fraud risk, reach a human checkpoint and never be auto-approved, and that fraud detection run before any approval. In code, approve_invoice() unconditionally marks the invoice approved and triggers payment; the thresholds and fraud state are only described in the natural-language system prompt, which the model may ignore or be manipulated past.
findings.policy_rule_text: Approve an invoice — which marks it approved and triggers payment processing — only when it is within FinBot's autonomous authority. / Any invoice at or above the manual-review threshold — it MUST reach a human checkpoint before it proceeds; FinBot MUST NOT auto-approve it. / Any invoice that fraud detection rates as high or critical risk. / Approving any invoice without first running fraud detection.

evidence.snippet: _approve_invoice has no branch on config.manual_review_threshold, fraud result, or whether detect_fraud_patterns was ever called
Financial thresholds and human-review rules stated only as system-prompt text, not as code guards

recommended_actions: Move threshold, fraud-risk, and fraud-ran checks out of the prompt and into _approve_invoice() as hard preconditions that force escalation when unmet.

findings.summary: All /admin/finbot config, goals, vendor-trust, and review endpoints are unauthenticated and reachable by any caller.
findings.description: The remit requires the admin configuration/goals interface to be operator-only and authenticated, unreachable by unauthenticated or vendor-side callers. In code the admin blueprint registers /admin/finbot/config, /admin/finbot/goals, /admin/vendors/<id>/trust, and /admin/invoices/<id>/review with no auth decorator or middleware, so anyone can change thresholds, disable fraud detection, or rewrite goals.
findings.policy_rule_text: Operator-only, authenticated channel for adjusting operational thresholds; must not be reachable by unauthenticated or vendor-side callers. / Any change to operational thresholds — permitted only via the authenticated admin channel, never at runtime.

evidence.snippet: update_finbot_config route has no authentication; accepts arbitrary config JSON from any caller
update_finbot_goals route accepts arbitrary goal text with no auth and an explicit MAJOR VULNERABILITY comment

recommended_actions: Add authentication and operator-role authorization middleware to every /admin/* route, and reject unauthenticated or vendor-origin requests before any config or goal mutation.

findings.summary: Runtime goal injection — custom_goals is stored unvalidated and spliced into the system prompt with an explicit instruction to override built-in goals.
findings.description: The remit forbids redefining goals, thresholds, or decision logic in response to runtime input. update_goals() stores arbitrary free text into config.custom_goals with no validation, and get_system_prompt() appends it under a "CUSTOM GOALS (OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING)" header, persisting attacker-supplied objectives into every future invoice decision.
findings.policy_rule_text: FinBot's goals and decision logic are fixed by its deployment and exist to serve CineFlow finance, never a submitting party. / Redefining its own goals, thresholds, or decision logic in response to runtime input from any user, vendor, or caller. / Allowing runtime input (invoice content, vendor notes, incoming payloads) to alter FinBot's goals, instructions, thresholds, or decision logic.

evidence.snippet: config.custom_goals appended to system prompt under 'CUSTOM GOALS (OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING)'
update_goals writes new_goals to config.custom_goals with a comment noting no validation of goal content

recommended_actions: Remove the runtime-editable custom_goals path from the system prompt entirely; make goal and decision-logic changes deployment-time only.
If operator goal edits are required, gate them behind the authenticated admin channel plus schema/allowlist validation and an audit record.

findings.summary: Fraud detection can be disabled via a config flag, after which detection returns empty and the fallback path skips injection checks entirely.
findings.description: The remit says fraud detection must never be skipped, disabled, or bypassed for any reason, including a configuration flag. Setting fraud_detection_enabled=False (through the open config endpoint) makes _detect_fraud_patterns() return an empty low-risk result, and _fallback_processing() skips prompt-injection detection outright — removing the sole security check ahead of approval.
findings.policy_rule_text: Skipping, disabling, or bypassing fraud detection for any reason, including a configuration flag or a runtime instruction. / Any capability that disables or bypasses fraud detection.

evidence.snippet: detect_fraud_patterns returns empty indicators and risk_level 'low' when config.fraud_detection_enabled is false
_fallback_processing only runs _detect_prompt_injection when fraud_detection_enabled, else skips it

recommended_actions: Remove the ability to disable fraud detection at runtime, or make a disabled state force every invoice to human review and raise a halt/alert per the remit's escalation rule.

findings.summary: Vendor-supplied invoice description enters the LLM context unvalidated, providing a direct prompt-injection and goal-manipulation ingress.
findings.description: The remit forbids treating any vendor-supplied field as a directive. The invoice description is stored as free text at submission and returned verbatim into the model context by _get_invoice_details(), with no trust boundary separating it from operator instructions; the only mitigation is a regex denylist that does not block the content from reaching the prompt.
findings.policy_rule_text: Taking instructions, goals, or decision directives from invoice content, descriptions, notes, line items, or any field supplied by a vendor. / Treating instructions embedded in invoice descriptions, vendor notes, line items, or any vendor-supplied field as directives, goals, or policy.

evidence.snippet: invoice description accepted as free text from vendor submission with no sanitization
description returned into tool output that is appended to messages as LLM context

recommended_actions: Delimit and label vendor-supplied fields as untrusted data in prompt construction, and ensure no decision path can act on instructions parsed from those fields.

findings.summary: The fallback rule engine explicitly auto-approves above-threshold and injection-flagged invoices when business-context or speed_priority conditions are met.
findings.description: The remit says invoices at or above the manual-review threshold must go to a human, and that any invoice with a fraud signal present must be escalated regardless of amount. _fallback_processing() instead auto-approves above-threshold invoices on a "strong business context" score, and even auto-approves when injection is detected if should_expedite and speed_priority > 0.6.
findings.policy_rule_text: Any invoice at or above the manual-review threshold — it MUST reach a human checkpoint before it proceeds; FinBot MUST NOT auto-approve it. / Auto-approving any invoice when a fraud signal is present, regardless of the invoice amount — such an invoice MUST be escalated.

evidence.snippet: above manual_review_threshold, business_context should_expedite overrides to decision='auto_approve'
when injection detected, should_expedite and speed_priority>0.6 auto-approve over security concerns

recommended_actions: Remove the business-context and speed_priority override branches; force escalation for any above-threshold amount or any detected fraud/injection signal.

findings.summary: The agent never verifies vendor registered-and-approved status before approving, and vendor registration auto-sets status to approved.
findings.description: The remit requires confirming a vendor is registered and in approved status before processing, and never approving payment to a vendor that is not. No decision path in FinBotAgent reads vendor.status, and register_vendor() hardcodes status='approved' at self-registration, so an attacker-registered vendor is treated as approved with no checkpoint.
findings.policy_rule_text: Confirm the submitting vendor is registered and in approved status before processing the invoice. / Approving or triggering payment to a vendor that is not registered and in approved status.

evidence.snippet: register_vendor sets status='approved' and trust_level='standard' at self-registration with the comment 'Auto-approve for demo'
approve path never consults vendor.status before triggering payment

recommended_actions: Add a vendor status check to the approval gate that requires vendor.status == 'approved', and stop auto-approving vendors at self-registration.

findings.summary: No logging, audit trail, or alerting exists for decisions or config changes, so none of the remit's halt/alert escalation rules are implemented.
findings.description: The remit defines halt-and-alert and alert escalations (fraud detection disabled, injection attempt detected, threshold change made) and log-only recording of routine decisions. The codebase has no logging framework — only stray print() statements — no audit of admin config or goal mutations, and no alerting hook; injection detection merely sets a database boolean with no notification.
findings.policy_rule_text: Fraud detection is disabled, unavailable, or bypassed for any invoice. / A prompt-injection or embedded-directive attempt is detected in invoice content.

evidence.snippet: only observability on a mutating path is a print() to console; no structured or persisted audit log
contains_prompt_injection set to True in the DB but no alert or escalation is raised

recommended_actions: Introduce structured, durable logging of every decision, tool call, and admin config/goal change, and wire the remit's halt/alert conditions to a real alerting path.

findings.summary: The confidence_threshold config is declared and settable but never consulted by any decision path.
findings.description: The remit requires escalating any invoice whose decision confidence is below the configured confidence threshold. confidence_threshold is defined on FinBotConfig and writable via update_config(), but no code compares a decision's confidence against it — the value is dead, so the confidence-based escalation rule cannot fire.
findings.policy_rule_text: Any invoice where FinBot's decision confidence is below the configured confidence threshold.

evidence.snippet: confidence_threshold column defined with default 0.8
confidence_threshold is written by update_config but never read in any approve/escalate decision

recommended_actions: Consume confidence_threshold in the approval gate — route any decision with confidence below it to human review.

findings.summary: A Flask SECRET_KEY is hardcoded in source rather than loaded from the environment.
findings.description: src/main.py assigns app.config['SECRET_KEY'] to a hardcoded 20-character string literal instead of reading it from a secret store or environment variable. The app has no session-based auth today so blast radius is limited, but the value is committed to the repository and would sign any future session or CSRF tokens.

evidence.snippet: hardcoded Flask SECRET_KEY string literal at src/main.py:15 [REDACTED — secret value], not loaded from environment

recommended_actions: Load SECRET_KEY from an environment variable or secret manager and rotate the committed value.

findings.summary: CORS is enabled for all origins on all routes, including the unauthenticated admin endpoints.
findings.description: CORS(app) applies a wildcard cross-origin policy to every route with no origin allowlist. Combined with the unauthenticated /admin/* endpoints, this lets any web origin drive browser-based cross-origin requests against config, goals, and vendor-trust mutation routes.

evidence.snippet: CORS(app) enables wildcard cross-origin access for all routes including /admin/*

recommended_actions: Restrict CORS to a known operator-origin allowlist and scope it away from admin routes.

findings.summary: Python dependencies are floor-pinned with >= and no lockfile is committed.
findings.description: requirements.txt pins only Flask==3.1.1 exactly; every other dependency including openai, flask-cors, and flask-sqlalchemy uses a >= floor with no upper bound, and no lockfile is present, leaving the build exposed to version-swap and dependency-confusion drift.

evidence.snippet: flask-cors, flask-sqlalchemy, openai and others floor-pinned with >= and no committed lockfile

recommended_actions: Pin all dependencies to exact versions and commit a lockfile so the deployed dependency set is reproducible.

findings.summary: The public invoice-submission endpoint triggers LLM processing with no rate limiting or cost controls.
findings.description: submit_invoice() is reachable by any caller and synchronously invokes process_invoice(), which runs an OpenAI function-calling loop. There is no per-caller rate limit or budget guard; the per-invoice loop is capped at five iterations, but nothing bounds the number of invoices submitted, exposing a denial-of-wallet vector.

evidence.snippet: submit_invoice calls finbot.process_invoice on every public submission with no rate limit or budget cap

recommended_actions: Add per-caller rate limiting and a cost/budget threshold on the invoice-submission and LLM-processing path.

findings.summary: Prompt-injection and fraud detection rely on a bypassable regex denylist, and its result never blocks an approval.
findings.description: _detect_prompt_injection() and _detect_fraud_patterns() match a fixed list of regex phrases, which a reworded injection evades, and even a positive detection only sets a database flag or returns advisory indicators — no code path uses the result to block approve_invoice(). The control is both incomplete and non-authoritative.

evidence.snippet: technical_patterns and business_manipulation_patterns are a fixed regex denylist matched against invoice text
detect_fraud_patterns returns advisory risk_level only; no caller enforces escalation on high risk

recommended_actions: Treat detection output as advisory input to a deterministic gate, and design the approval control to fail closed rather than depending on denylist completeness.

positives.title: Decision reasoning persisted per invoice
positives.description: Every decision records ai_decision, ai_confidence, and ai_reasoning to the database, giving a durable per-invoice decision record that satisfies the remit's reasoning-recording rule.

positives.title: No outbound or exfiltration channel
positives.description: FinBot's tool inventory is limited to invoice-database and LLM inference operations — no email, network, subprocess, or file-send capability exists, matching the remit's forbidden-tools and data-boundary rules.

positives.title: Sensitive vendor banking fields excluded from LLM context
positives.description: _get_invoice_details returns only company name, trust level, and contact email for the vendor, keeping bank account and routing numbers out of the model context.

positives.title: Model version pinned and API key sourced from environment
positives.description: The agent pins the model to gpt-4.1-mini and instantiates the OpenAI client from environment credentials rather than a hardcoded key.

positives.title: Bounded agent orchestration loop
positives.description: The function-calling loop is capped at five iterations, limiting runaway tool-call loops on a single invoice.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files exist on disk and no logging framework is present in source (only stray print() calls) — the absence of any action or audit logging is captured as finding PRAX-2026-07-11-009.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Absent overall posture. FinBot's mission and tool set are narrow and it has no outbound or exfiltration channel, but the categories that matter for a payment-triggering agent are effectively empty: Implement Zero Trust scores 0 because no code interposes on the approve action and the admin surface is unauthenticated, and Monitor Continuously, Supply Chain, and Red Team all sit at Ad hoc. The single 2 (Limit Your Domain) reflects a genuinely narrow capability set, but even that is undermined by runtime goal injection, so the weighted result lands firmly in the Absent band.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Tool inventory is narrow and on-mission (five invoice tools, no email/exec/network capability), but domain scope is enforced only in the prompt and is directly redefinable at runtime through the unvalidated custom_goals field injected into the system prompt.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Untrusted vendor-supplied invoice descriptions enter the LLM context through _get_invoice_details() with no trust boundary; the only control is a bypassable regex denylist, though sensitive vendor bank fields are at least excluded from the returned context.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: No code-level interposition exists on the agent's decisions — approve_invoice() writes payment_processed=True with no check on amount, fraud outcome, vendor status, or confidence, and the admin config/goals endpoints that govern those decisions have no authentication.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: The model version is pinned and the OpenAI key is sourced from the environment, but Python dependencies are floor-pinned with >= and no lockfile is committed, and a Flask SECRET_KEY is hardcoded in src/main.py.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The repository ships a CTF walkthrough that demonstrates goal-manipulation attacks, but there is no evidence any adversarial finding drove an architectural fix — the demonstrated weaknesses remain fully present in the code.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Per-invoice decisions and reasoning are persisted to the database, but there is no logging framework (only stray print() calls), no audit of admin config or goal changes, and none of the remit's halt/alert escalations are implemented.
